ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for flavoured bubble tea with milk and no topping; A0FKP (tea-based beverages with milk) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for bubble tea as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium cup of flavoured bubble tea with milk and no topping is approximately 500 g, representing a standard single-serve portion as sold at Singaporean bubble tea shops.

nutrient_source.title: Flavoured bubble tea with milk (no topping) — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for flavoured bubble tea with milk (no topping), cr_id D08010030. All per-100g values are sourced at confidence 0.95 as recorded in the input metadata.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — sugar-sweetened milk tea beverages analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for flavoured bubble tea with milk (no topping) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 70 is estimated by analogy to sweetened milk tea beverages (GI ~65–75) reported in Atkinson et al. 2021 and peer-reviewed literature on sugar-sweetened tea drinks. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 55.3) / 100 = 38.7, rounded to 38.5 using 11.06 g/100g × 500 g serving = 55.3 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (11.06 g/100 g) classifying this as a sugar-sweetened beverage, which independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines, despite low sodium (13 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this beverage due to its SSB classification; requesting 0% sugar is the minimum mitigation, and non-dairy creamer saturated fat may further compound cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 11.06 g sugar/100 g, zero dietary fibre, and a high GI of 70, producing rapid postprandial glucose excursions; liquid sugar is absorbed faster than equivalent solid carbohydrate.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage in its standard formulation; if consumed, request 0% sugar and limit portion to minimise glycaemic impact, noting that even reduced-sugar versions may contain residual sweeteners.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sugar load (11.06 g/100 g) with likely saturated fat from non-dairy creamer, combined with SSB classification, contributes to dyslipidaemia, visceral adiposity, and elevated triglycerides relevant to CVD risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of this beverage; the combination of added sugar and non-dairy creamer fat is associated with adverse lipid profiles and increased CVD event risk per AHA dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Flavoured bubble tea with milk (no topping) is a freshly prepared Taiwanese-origin beverage widely sold across Singapore and Southeast Asia. It consists of a flavoured tea base — commonly fruit-flavoured or taro — blended with milk or non-dairy creamer and sweetened with sugar syrup, served without any additional toppings such as tapioca pearls, pudding, or jelly. The drink is typically shaken or stirred and served cold over ice. The texture is smooth and milky with a sweet, flavoured tea character. Per 100 g, it provides 65 kcal, 0.33 g protein, 2.41 g fat, and 11.06 g carbohydrate (all as sugar), with negligible dietary fibre (0.0 g) and low sodium (13 mg). The high sugar content (11.06 g/100 g) classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB), triggering clinical concern for Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. The non-dairy creamer component likely contributes saturated fat. The GI is estimated at 70 (high), and a standard 500 ml serving delivers approximately 55 g of carbohydrate with a glycaemic load of ~38.5. This beverage is not recommended for individuals managing diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease in its standard sweetened formulation. Requesting 0% sugar is the primary mitigation strategy.
common_user_queries: How many calories in bubble tea with milk?
Is bubble tea high in sugar?
Is milk tea good for hypertension?
Is bubble tea suitable for heart patients?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D08010030) at confidence 0.95. This is an exact match from the primary approved regional source for Singapore foods. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature (University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021) as no direct measurement exists for this specific product.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this freshly prepared flavoured bubble tea with milk (no topping) are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (cr_id D08010030) as of 2026. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 as confirmed by the HPB source — this beverage contains no fibre-contributing ingredients in its base formulation (no toppings). The input health_suitability entries referenced 'rheumatoid_arthritis' and 'trans fat 0.1g/100g'; trans fat data is not confirmed in the HPB source and has been set to -1 pending verification. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on sweetened milk tea beverages in Atkinson et al. 2021 (Am J Clin Nutr); no direct GI measurement for this specific product was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycaemic load per serving calculated using 500 g serving size: (70 × 55.3 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 38.7, reported as 38.5. Halal status assumed based on typical Singapore bubble tea shop formulations; kosher status not verified. The input record listed 'breakfast' as an occasion tag; this has been retained in meal_context but is atypical — bubble tea is more commonly consumed as an afternoon or social beverage.
